content.problemStatement:
Let GGG be a simple connected undirected graph with NNN vertices.

Consider the following pseudocode, that produces one DFS order of GGG:

`mark = [0, ..., 0] is an array of length N+1 filled with zeros
order = [] is an empty array

function DFS(v):
	append v to order
	mark[v] = 1
	for x in 1..n:
		if x is adjacent to v and mark[x] == 0:
			DFS(x)

DFS(1)
`Quite simply, this is a depth-first search starting at vertex 111, that processes adjacencies in ascending order.

`order` denotes the order in which vertices were visited during this process.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN, that contains each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.

It is guaranteed that A1=1A_1 = 1A1‚Äč=1.

Count the number of graphs GGG on NNN vertices such that running the above algorithm would result in exactly AAA being the returned DFS order.

The answer can be large, so report it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the number of nodes in the graph.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers - A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of undirected graphs for which AAA is the DFS order obtained by the given algorithm, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Ai‚Č†AjA_i \ne A_jAi‚ÄčÓÄ†=Aj‚Äč for all 1‚Č§i<j‚Č§N1 \le i \lt j \le N1‚Č§i<j‚Č§N

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Only the graph {(1,2)}\{(1, 2)\}{(1,2)} is valid and it produces the required DFS order.
Test case 222: The graphs {(1,2),(1,3)}\{(1, 2), (1, 3)\}{(1,2),(1,3)}, {(1,2),(1,3),(2,3)}\{(1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3)\}{(1,2),(1,3),(2,3)} and {(1,2),(2,3)}\{(1, 2), (2, 3)\}{(1,2),(2,3)} are valid and produce the necessary DFS order.
